title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1 yrSqliteViewer
An “all in one” html file for viewing sqlite databases.

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1.1 What is yrSqliteViewer
I created yrSqliteViewer out of frustration when trying to teach the basics of SQL to computer novices. I decided to focus on SQLite databases since a server is not needed. I wanted something that could be run in a web browser without students needing to install any other software. I did this quickly by “vibe-coding” yrSqliteViewer with Cursor in the 2023/2024 timeframe.
This product is intended for teaching/learning purposes and is not intended a “production” level product. I recommend using small databases (i.e. a few tables and not an overwhelming amount of data in each table).
Alternatives are packages such as DB Browser or SQLite Viewer. However, I needed to support students with all sorts of computers (windows, mac, chromebook, etc) and I didn’t want to spend a lot of time teaching a particular software package. I wanted to focus on the SQL commands. I thought a browser interface would be best and I couldn’t find anything that I wanted to use. If something else is available when you’re reading this, perhaps try it - and let me know about it :)
FYI - the “yr” in the name “yrSqliteViewer” are my initials. I know the name doesn’t have great marketing appeal but there already was a product named “Sqlite Viewer” :)

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1.2 Quick Start
To get started quickly do the following:

Navigate to https://github.com/y-rosenthal/yrSqliteViewer and download the following file (this is the only file you really need).

yrSqliteViewer.html NOTE: Due to technical considerations to download this file right click on the link and choose “Save Link As” or “Save As”. The other files below can be downloaded by just clicking on the links.

After downloading the file double-click on the file to open it in your browser. (I recommend using Chrome. Other browsers should also work fine but they haven’t been tested as well).
Download these additiional file (optional).

sampleDatabase-books-v025.sqlite - sample sqlite database (you can use any sqlite database file)
yrSqliteViewerSession-sample.yrslv - sample session file (see below)

open yrSqliteViewer.html with a web browser. Probably best to use Chrome. You can try other browsers but they weren’t tested as well as Chrome was.
Press the “Load Database File” button and choose the file sampleDatabase-books-v025.sqlite that you downloaded above. Wait a few seconds for the database file to load.
Click the “Show Rows (for all tables)” button to see the rows in the tables.
Click the “Show Structure (for all tables)” button to see the structure of the tables.
Type a SQL select statement - e.g. select  from title where price < 20* - in the box entitled “SQL Command 1”. Then press the “Run Query” button.
Type another SQL select statement - e.g. select pub_name, city from publishers - in the box entitled “SQL Command 2”, etc.
Press the “Save Session” button (at the top of the page). Specifiy a filename. This file will contain both the database file you were using as well as the queries that you have run.
If you followed the instructions in the previous step, you can restore the session that you saved. Alternatively, you can restore the sample session that is saved in the file yrSqliteViewerSession-sample.yrslv that you downloaded in the first step above.
Restoring a session reloads the database as well as any SQL statements that were in the interface when the session file was saved.
To restore a session, close your browser. Then open the yrSqliteViewer.html file again in the browser. Press the “Restore Session” button and choose the file that contains the saved session (i.e. either the file you specified in the previous step or the file yrSqliteViewerSession-sample.yrslv that you downloaded in the first step above. This opens the database as well as restores the SQL statements that were saved with the session.

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1.3 Features
The current version has the following features:

load a sqlite database
view the structure of the database (column names and datatypes) without running ANY SQL commands
view the data in the database (i.e. the actual rows) without running ANY SQL commands
execute SELECT statements against the loaded database
save a session and restore a session The session is saved in a file with a .yrslv extension. If you open a saved session (by pressing “Restore Session” button) you will automatically load the database file as well as any SQL commands that were typed when you saved the session file.

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
section: 1.4 NOT currently supported (but may be in the future)
text:
1.4 NOT currently supported (but may be in the future)
There are MANY additional features that could be added to this. The following are NOT currently supported but may be added in the future:

the current version does NOT support sql commands other than SELECT (e.g. update, delete, etc)
reordering the existing queries in the interface
better support for large databases
showing the “entity relationship diagram” (either via image files or mermaid ERDs or similar)
many other features …

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1.5 Sample database (sampleDatabase-books-v025.sqlite)
There is a sample database included in the repo (sampleDatabase-books-v025.sqlite). The “-v025” is my own version number of this sqlite file - I’ve been using this database to teach my classes for several years and I’ve modified the database slightly every now and then.
There is also a “session” file (yrSqliteViewerSession-sample.yrslv) in the repo that contains this database along with a few sample queries (see explanation of session files in the features section above).
The sample database is derived from the database that was used in the following book. It is a wonderful book for learning SQL but unfortunately it seems to currently be out of print.

SQL: Visual QuickStart Guide, 3rd Edition By Chris Fehily Published Jun 4, 2008 by Peachpit Press

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1.6 About the code
The code uses Pyodide to enable running Python directly in a browser. The code then uses standard Python packages to interact with the sqlite files.
The code is very messy. It was “vibe-coded” circal 2023/2024. It worked for my purposes but attempts to improve the functionality became difficult with just “vibe coding”. I didn’t have time to focus on turning this into a real product (I started but didn’t have time to focus on this).
In the time since I first coded this, vibe coding has gotten a LOT better. Claude Code Max is currently amazing (June 2025) and could possibly recreate this with a few prompts. However, I don’t have time now to focus on a new implementation. Simple changes could probably be done with the existing code base without too much difficulty. If you want to add a lot more functionality, I recommend possibly starting from scratch.

title: yrSqliteViewer Documentation
text:
1.7 Download older versions of yrSqliteViewer.html
If you need an older version (for example if a newer version has a bug), use these steps:

Go to the repo on GitHub: https://github.com/y-rosenthal/yrSqliteViewer
Click the folder named archived-versions.
Click the specific version file you want (for example yrSqliteViewer-v0.0.2.html).
On that file page, click the Raw button.
When the raw file opens in your browser, right-click and choose Save As… (or Save Page As…).
Save the file to your computer, then double-click it to open it in your browser.

Direct link to the current archived version:

https://github.com/y-rosenthal/yrSqliteViewer/raw/master/archived-versions/yrSqliteViewer-v0.0.2.html

title: 2  yrSqliteViewer Developer Guide
text:
3 Purpose
This guide documents how version files are maintained so students and instructors can always access prior working copies of yrSqliteViewer.html from GitHub without using git.


4 Versioning approach

The main application file is yrSqliteViewer.html.
The visible version string in the UI should follow semantic style (for example 0.0.3).
Archived copies are stored in archived-versions/.
Archive filenames use this pattern:

yrSqliteViewer-v<version>.html
Example:
yrSqliteViewer-v0.0.2.html


5 Archive policy (important)
When yrSqliteViewer.html changes and you are preparing a commit:

Determine the currently committed version (the one in HEAD before your new commit).
Copy that committed file into archived-versions/ using the filename pattern above.
Make sure the new development copy (yrSqliteViewer.html) has the next intended version label.
Update links/instructions in README.md if needed.
Commit both:

the new main yrSqliteViewer.html
the new archived file in archived-versions/


This keeps an easily downloadable history in the repository itself.


6 Current archived structure

Folder name: archived-versions
Example archived file already added:

archived-versions/yrSqliteViewer-v0.0.2.html




7 Why this is required

Most students are non-technical and may not be able to run git commands.
If a breaking change is accidentally introduced, students can quickly download a prior version directly from GitHub.
Instructors can distribute a known-stable file with a specific versioned filename.



8 Suggested release checklist
Before publishing a new version:

Open and smoke test yrSqliteViewer.html in Chrome.
Confirm key features still work:

load database
run SELECT query
save session
restore session

Add prior committed version file to archived-versions/.
Confirm README download links are correct.
Commit and push.



9 User-facing download path
Students can get archived files from:

Repo root: https://github.com/y-rosenthal/yrSqliteViewer
Folder: archived-versions

Direct raw-file pattern:
https://github.com/y-rosenthal/yrSqliteViewer/raw/master/archived-versions/yrSqliteViewer-v<version>.html

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
4 What this tool is
yrSqliteViewer is a single HTML file that lets you open and explore SQLite databases in a web browser.
You do not need to install database software.

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
8.1 See all tables
Use the interface section that shows database tables after loading your file.

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
8.2 View table data quickly
Use buttons such as:

Show Rows (for all tables)
Show Structure (for all tables)

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
8.3 Run a query
Type a query like:
select * from title where price < 20;
Then press Run Query.

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
9.1 Save session

Click Save Session.
Choose a filename.
A .yrslv file is downloaded.

This session file includes:

your loaded database
your SQL commands in the interface

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
9.2 Restore session

Open yrSqliteViewer.html in your browser.
Click Restore Session.
Select your .yrslv file.

Your database and SQL text boxes are restored.

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
section: 11.1 The file opens as text, not as an app
text:
11.1 The file opens as text, not as an app

Make sure the filename ends with .html
Re-download and use Save As

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
text:
11.2 Query does not run

Check that your SQL is a SELECT statement
Verify table names and column names
Make sure a database is loaded first

title: 3  USER-GUIDE-0.0.2-v001.qmd
section: 11.3 Nothing happens after loading a large database
text:
11.3 Nothing happens after loading a large database

Wait a little longer; large files can take time
Start with a smaller database when learning

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
5 Document purpose
This document is a rebuild specification for the current yrSqliteViewer system.
Goal: enable a modern AI coding workflow to recreate the existing product behavior as-is (including quirks), then evolve it safely.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
7.1 What the app is

A browser-based SQLite viewer/query runner.
Designed for non-technical teaching/learning use.
Operates by loading a local SQLite file into an in-browser Python runtime (Pyodide).
Supports saving and restoring sessions that bundle:

SQLite DB file
SQL query text/history
Stored query result snapshots

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
7.2 What the app is not

Not a production-grade DB admin tool.
Not a multi-user app.
Not currently a full SQL editor with advanced object management.
Not currently a true offline-first packaged app (depends on CDN assets in current implementation).

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
8.1 External browser dependencies (CDN)

Pyodide v0.23.0
JSZip 3.10.1
FileSaver.js 2.0.5
CodeMirror 5.65.2 core + SQL mode + eclipse theme

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
8.2 Core runtime model

User selects .sqlite/.db file.
File is loaded into browser memory and written to Pyodide FS as database.sqlite.
Python sqlite3 executes metadata queries and user SQL.
Results are serialized to JSON and returned to JS for rendering.
UI maintains query cards and output cards in DOM.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
8.3 State variables (JS)

pyodideInstance: singleton runtime, lazy-initialized.
queryHistory: array of query objects with query, timestamp, results.
queryInterfaceCount: current query card count tracker.
currentDatabaseName: active DB filename.
tableNames: table list from schema scan.
isSessionSaved: dirty-state flag used by beforeunload.
editors: map of CodeMirror instances keyed by sqlQuery<id>.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
8.4 High-level flow
flowchart TD
  appLoad[AppLoaded] --> chooseDb[ChooseSQLiteFile]
  chooseDb --> initPyodide[InitPyodideIfNeeded]
  initPyodide --> writeFs[Writedatabase.sqlite]
  writeFs --> inspectSchema[ReadTablesAndColumns]
  inspectSchema --> renderTableCards[RenderTableCards]
  renderTableCards --> showDefaultStructure[ShowStructureForAllTables]
  showDefaultStructure --> queryWork[UserRunsQueries]
  queryWork --> saveSession[SaveSessionToYRSLV]
  queryWork --> restoreSession[RestoreSessionFromYRSLV]

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
9.1 Global page layout
Order from top to bottom:

Control panel
Database tables header + global table controls
Initial no-file instructional message (hidden after DB load)
Loading message area
Dynamic table cards area
SQL commands header + global SQL font controls
Dynamic SQL command cards area

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
9.2 Control panel contents

Title text: yrSqliteViewer (ver 0.0.3) (current working file).
Small instructions link to repository.
Load Database File pseudo-button (hidden file input label).
Selected filename text (no file chosen yet by default).
Save Session button.
Restore Session pseudo-button (hidden file input label).
Clear Session button.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
9.3 Database tables section
Header: Database Tables.
Controls:

Show Structure (for all tables)
Show Rows (for all tables)
Global font controls for table cards:

A-, A+, numeric input (range clamped 0.5 to 3)


Dynamic content:

One card per table.
Each card has:

Table: <name>
table-local A-, A+
Show Structure
Show Rows
output region (<tableName>_info)

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
9.4 SQL commands section
Header: SQL Commands.
Controls:

Global query interface font controls:

A-, A+, numeric input (range clamped 0.5 to 3)


Dynamic content:

Starts with one query card labeled SQL Command 1.
Each query card has:

local A-, A+
CodeMirror SQL editor area
Run Query button
output region

Auto-growth behavior:

After running a non-empty query in last card, app auto-adds a new blank query card.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.1 Load database behavior
Trigger:

File input event on sqliteFile.

Expected behavior:

If no file selected:

Show error in output area: Please select a file.

Hide no-file message, show loading message.
If not restoring:

Reset SQL card area to one fresh query card.

Initialize Pyodide/sqlite3 if first run.
Write uploaded bytes to database.sqlite.
Query sqlite metadata:

SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';
PRAGMA table_info(<table>)

Render table cards and default each card to structure view.
Hide loading message.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.2 Query execution behavior
Trigger:

Run Query button on a card.
Keyboard shortcut Ctrl-Enter or Cmd-Enter in CodeMirror.

Behavior:

If Pyodide not initialized: return silently.
Read SQL text from relevant CodeMirror editor.
If blank after trim: return silently.
Execute query in Python:

cursor.execute("""<query>""")
columns = [description[0] for description in cursor.description]
rows = cursor.fetchall()

Render HTML table output.
Update or append matching queryHistory entry by card index.
Mark isSessionSaved = false.
Ensure there is always one blank trailing query card.

Important current limitation:

The code assumes cursor.description exists.
For non-SELECT commands, cursor.description may be None, causing error output.
This is one reason current product effectively behaves as SELECT-oriented.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.3 Table structure and row display

10.3.1 Structure

showTableStructure(tableName, columns) renders a two-column table:

Column
Type




10.3.2 Rows

showTableData(tableName) executes:

SELECT * FROM '<tableName>'

Renders all rows in a scrollable HTML table.
On error, shows red error message in table info panel.



10.3.3 Bulk operations

showAllTablesStructure() loops all table cards and reconstructs column metadata by parsing each structure button onclick string (string-parsing based; fragile but current behavior).
showAllTablesData() loops table cards and awaits row loading table-by-table.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.4 Font-size controls
Implemented at three levels:

Per query editor wrapper
Per table card
Global for all table cards / all query cards

Current clamp behavior:

Per-card adjustments in adjustFontSize: 0.5 to 2 for specific target handling.
Global setter/adjusters: typically 0.5 to 3.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.5 Session save behavior (.yrslv)
Trigger:

Save Session button.

Flow:

Prompt for base filename (default yrSqliteViewerSession).
Rebuild queryHistory from current editor contents:

Saves non-empty query cards only.
Preserves prior results when available.

Build JSON metadata file.
Build TXT summary file.
Read current database.sqlite from Pyodide FS.
Zip JSON + TXT + DB into one .yrslv.
Download with FileSaver.
Mark session saved.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.6 Session restore behavior (.yrslv)
Trigger:

Restore Session file input change.

Flow:

Read archive with JSZip.
Locate .json entry.
Parse and validate:

jsonFormatVersion must equal "0.1".

Extract DB by databaseName.
Write DB into Pyodide FS.
Load database via loadDatabase(file, true) (restore mode).
Recreate query cards from session queries.
Re-hydrate editor text and stored results tables.
Add one extra blank query card at end.
Reset restore input value for reselecting same file.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.7 Clear session behavior
Trigger:

Clear Session button.

Flow:

Confirmation dialog.
Reset in-memory state.
Clear table list.
Reset to one query card.
Reset selected filename label.
Re-show initial no-file instructional message.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
10.8 Unsaved-change warning

window.onbeforeunload prompts user if:

queryHistory.length > 0
and isSessionSaved === false

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
11.1 Container

Extension: .yrslv
Actual format: ZIP archive

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
11.2 Required archive members

One JSON metadata file (name usually <baseFilename>.json)
One TXT summary file (name usually <baseFilename>.txt)
One SQLite file (name equals databaseName)

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
14.1 Screenshots currently available
The following screenshots are currently available from this documentation workflow (not the app behavior walkthrough):

14.1.2 Earlier broken README link behavior (for historical context)

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
section: 14.2 TODO capture runbook for remaining app screenshots
text:
14.2 TODO capture runbook for remaining app screenshots
The screenshots below still need to be captured from yrSqliteViewer.html. Use this exact runbook to keep captures reproducible.

14.2.1 01-initial-no-db.png

Open yrSqliteViewer.html in Chrome.
Do not load any file.
Ensure the instructional no-file message is visible.
Capture full-page screenshot.
Save as _doc-assets/spec-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001/01-initial-no-db.png.



14.2.2 02-db-loaded-table-cards.png

Click Load Database File.
Select sampleDatabase-books-v025.sqlite.
Wait for loading indicator to disappear.
Confirm table cards are visible.
Capture screenshot and save as 02-db-loaded-table-cards.png.



14.2.3 03-table-structure.png

Starting from loaded DB state.
In one table card, click Show Structure.
Ensure the Column / Type structure table is visible.
Capture screenshot and save as 03-table-structure.png.



14.2.4 04-table-rows.png

Starting from loaded DB state.
In one table card, click Show Rows.
Ensure row data grid is visible in that table card.
Capture screenshot and save as 04-table-rows.png.



14.2.5 05-multi-query-output.png

In SQL Command 1, enter:

select * from titles limit 5;

Click Run Query.
In the next SQL command card, enter:

select pub_name, city from publishers;

Click Run Query.
Ensure at least two query outputs are visible at once.
Capture screenshot and save as 05-multi-query-output.png.



14.2.6 06-save-session.png (optional if save dialog capture is OS-restricted)

Click Save Session.
Enter filename spec-save-check and confirm.
If OS blocks dialog capture, capture immediately after save with button state/context.
Save as 06-save-session.png.



14.2.7 07-restore-session.png

Click Restore Session.
Select yrSqliteViewerSession-sample.yrslv.
Wait until SQL cards and outputs are restored.
Confirm multiple restored SQL commands are visible.
Capture screenshot and save as 07-restore-session.png.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
15.1 Functional smoke tests

Load sampleDatabase-books-v025.sqlite.
Confirm table list appears.
Confirm “Show Structure (for all tables)” renders all structure sections.
Confirm “Show Rows (for all tables)” renders all row tables.
Run at least 3 SELECT queries in different cards.
Confirm automatic blank next SQL card appears.
Save session as .yrslv.
Reload app and restore session.
Confirm query text and prior results are restored.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
15.2 Compatibility tests

Chrome latest (primary target, existing recommendation).
At least one other Chromium-based browser.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
15.3 Data fidelity tests

Compare row/column counts for representative tables between original and rebuilt app.
Validate restored session JSON schema and content fields.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
16.1 M1: Full SQLite command support

16.1.1 Goal
Support full SQLite command set (DDL, DML, PRAGMA, transaction control), not only SELECT-style outputs.


16.1.2 Requirements

Execute commands like:

CREATE TABLE
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
ALTER TABLE
DROP
PRAGMA
transaction commands

For non-result commands, display structured success metadata:

rows affected
last insert rowid (if available)
execution time

Allow multi-statement execution with clear per-statement status/output blocks.
Add safe-guard mode option for teaching:

read-only mode toggle
confirm destructive statements

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
section: 16.2 M2: Tabbed collections of SQL command windows
text:
16.2 M2: Tabbed collections of SQL command windows

16.2.1 Goal
Allow grouping SQL command cards into named tab collections; support multiple collections.


16.2.2 Requirements

Introduce “workspace tabs” (collection-level tabs).
Each collection contains multiple SQL command cards.
User can:

create/delete/rename collection tabs
reorder collections
duplicate collection
move SQL cards between collections

Session format must persist full tab/collection structure.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
section: 16.3 M3: Expand/collapse for teaching visibility control
text:
16.3 M3: Expand/collapse for teaching visibility control

16.3.1 Goal
Enable quick focus while preserving ability to view many outputs at once.


16.3.2 Requirements

Table area:

Each table card must have collapse/expand for output panel.
Global expand/collapse all for table outputs.

SQL area:

Each SQL card must have independent collapse/expand for:

query editor panel
output panel

Global controls for expand/collapse all SQL editors and outputs.

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
section: 16.4 M4: Mermaid ER diagrams in sessions
text:
16.4 M4: Mermaid ER diagrams in sessions

16.4.1 Goal
Allow saving/rendering ER diagrams as Mermaid text with session portability.


16.4.2 Requirements

Add Mermaid editor panel(s) in UI.
Support rendering Mermaid diagrams inline.
Persist diagrams in session archive JSON.
Restore diagrams exactly on session load.
Include export options:

Mermaid source text
rendered image (optional future enhancement)

title: 4  SPEC-refactor-same-functionality-0.0.4-v001.qmd
text:
16.5 M5: Multi-target architecture (Next.js + offline options)

16.5.1 Goal
Build a modern app architecture that supports:

Next.js connected mode
Disconnected desktop app (Electron or similar) for macOS + Windows
Optional pure .html mode (possibly SELECT-only)



16.5.2 Requirements

Define shared core domain package:

SQL execution abstraction
session schema
query history model
table metadata model

Ensure cross-target session compatibility:

same session format family
forward/backward compatibility strategy

Connected mode can add online-only features later without breaking offline core.
Desktop app must function fully disconnected.
Pure HTML mode may have constrained feature set (acceptable).



16.5.3 Suggested target architecture
flowchart LR
  sharedCore[SharedCoreDomain] --> nextApp[NextJsApp]
  sharedCore --> desktopApp[ElectronDesktopApp]
  sharedCore --> htmlLite[StandaloneHtmlLite]
  nextApp --> sessionFmt[SharedSessionFormat]
  desktopApp --> sessionFmt
  htmlLite --> sessionFmt
